text: Integrating spatial transcriptomics data across different conditions, technologies and developmental stages. Xiang Zhou, Kangning Dong, Shihua Zhang. Nature Computational Science. Published online: 12 October 2023. Check for updates. Abstract: With the rapid generation of spatial transcriptomics (ST) data, integrative analysis of multiple ST datasets from different conditions, technologies and developmental stages is becoming increasingly important. Here we present a graph attention neural network called STAligner for integrating and aligning ST datasets, enabling spatially aware data integration, simultaneous spatial domain identification and downstream comparative analysis. We apply STAligner to ST datasets of the human cortex slices from different samples, the mouse olfactory bulb slices generated by two profiling technologies, the mouse hippocampus tissue slices under normal and Alzheimer’s disease conditions, and the spatiotemporal atlases of mouse organogenesis. STAligner efficiently captures the shared tissue structures across different slices, the disease-related substructures and the dynamical changes during mouse embryonic development. In addition, the shared spatial domain and nearest-neighbor pairs identified by STAligner can be further considered as corresponding pairs to guide the three-dimensional reconstruction of consecutive slices, achieving more accurate local structure-guided registration than the existing method. Introduction: In multicellular organisms, cells are deeply influenced by their tissue environment. Knowledge of the spatial position of cells has a critical role in understanding why cells from different locations have distinctive structures and functions. Recent spatial transcriptomics (ST) technologies provide new ways to measure messenger RNA expression and corresponding spatial coordinates in tissues. Various ST technologies have been developed with different spatial resolutions. For example, the spatial barcoding-based method 10x Visium achieves a spatial resolution of 55 μm with each capture site (spot) containing approximately 1–10 cells. Slide-seq improves spatial resolution to near-cellular level (10 μm) and Stereo-seq further reaches subcellular resolution (0.22 μm). Indeed, these technologies have been widely used to explore the biological architecture of tissues, the biomarkers of complex disease and the development process of organogenesis. In particular, computational methods have been successfully applied to ST data of a single condition, technology or developmental stage to characterize the tissue structure, identify spatially variable genes and decipher spatial cell–cell communication patterns. However, integrated analysis of ST datasets produced by different conditions, technologies or developmental stages remains challenging. Identifying the shared and specific spatial domains (defined as regions with similar spatial expression patterns) is one of the fundamental tasks for the integrative analysis of multiple ST datasets. Several methods have been proposed to address this issue in single-slice ST datasets by incorporating spatial information and gene expression profiles. For example, STAGATE uses a deep learning model and leverages the graph attention mechanism to learn the spatial heterogeneity. However, integrative analysis of multiple ST datasets poses a unique challenge, as the batch effects across different datasets may mask the actual biological signals and hamper data integration. Compared with single-slice ST data analytics for deciphering spatial heterogeneity, integrative analysis of multiple ST datasets can provide more comprehensive characterizations of spatial tissue structures. For example, integrating slices from normal and diseased tissue can facilitate the identification of disease-specific spatial domains. However, most current integration analyzes either pool gene expression data across slices or employs existing batch correction methods developed for single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data, such as Harmony and Seurat V3, without considering the spatial coordinates. An unsupervised spatial embedding method, SEDR, was combined with Harmony to embed ST datasets for batch correction. As these two modules are optimized independently, their combined performance is limited. A recently developed method, PASTE, integrates adjacent slices into a center slice to improve the clustering performance of spatial domains and achieve three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction. However, PASTE requires that adjacent slices are biological replicates with global and rigid similarity, which is often violated in heterogeneous tissue. To this end, we develop a graph attention neural network, named STAligner, for integrating and aligning multiple ST datasets. STAligner takes the normalized gene expression and spatial coordinates of multiple slices as input, and produces batch-corrected spot embeddings, enabling consensus spatial domain identification, downstream comparative analysis and 3D tissue reconstruction. We demonstrate improved performance of STAligner on diverse datasets from different individuals, technologies, disease conditions and developmental stages.

section: Overview, Results on Same Platform, and Human DLPFC Benchmark
text: Overview of STAligner: STAligner takes the normalized gene expression and spatial coordinates of ST data from multiple tissue slices as input. For each ST slice, STAligner constructs a spatial neighbor graph between spots using the spatial coordinates. STAligner employs a graph attention autoencoder on the spatial neighbor graph, and constructs the spot triplets based on current embeddings to guide the integration and alignment process of different slices by attracting similar spots and discriminating dissimilar spots across slices. STAligner defines the triplet that contains anchor–positive and anchor–negative spot pairs. The former is defined as the mutual nearest neighbors (MNNs) with similar gene expressions but belong to two different slices, and the latter belongs to the same slice with different spatial positions and dissimilar expressions. STAligner introduces the triplet loss to update the spot embedding to reduce the distance from the anchor spot to the positive one, and increase the distance from the anchor spot to the negative one. The graph attention autoencoder training and triplet construction are optimized iteratively until batch-corrected embeddings are generated. STAligner can be applied to integrate ST datasets to achieve alignment and simultaneous identification of spatial domains from different biological samples, technological platforms, developmental (embryonic) stages, disease conditions and consecutive slices of a tissue for 3D slice alignment. Results: Improved integration on slices from the same platform. To quantitatively evaluate the integration performance of STAligner, we applied it to the human dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) ST dataset generated by 10x Visium. It consists of 12 tissue slices from 3 adult samples with 4 adjacent slices for each. The original study has manually annotated 6 neocortical layers from layer 1 to layer 6 and the white matter (WM). By taking the manual annotations as ground truth, we evaluated the performance of STAligner on simultaneous identification of spatial domains and compared it with that of the non-spatial integration method Harmony and two recently developed spatial integration approaches SEDR and PASTE in terms of the adjusted Rand index (ARI). We first evaluated the performance on adjacent slices of a single sample and observed that both STAligner and SEDR can effectively integrate the four slices with matched low-dimensional embeddings, and the common cortical layers across slices were evenly mixed, and different layers were separated and ordered with spatial structure from layer 1 to layer 6 and white matter. Although Harmony cannot separate the cortical layers, its embeddings of the four slices were mixed. PASTE captures the layer structures but the slices were not evenly mixed as it did not perform embedding alignment explicitly. STAligner achieved the highest ARI scores among all methods for the three samples. For example, STAligner accurately captured the layer boundaries and obtained a higher clustering accuracy (ARI = 0.61) than that of Harmony (ARI = 0.28), PASTE (ARI = 0.36) and SEDR (ARI = 0.57) for sample 1. The layer structure identified by the non-spatial approach Harmony suffered from serious noisy boundary issues and could only identify the layer 3 and white matter structure correctly. For comparison, the spatially aware embedding methods could more accurately capture tissue architecture heterogeneity and the obtained domain boundary was notably smoother. Similar conclusions can also be found for the other two samples, indicating the importance of considering spatial information for integration of multiple ST slices even from the same tissue sample. We also found that STAligner can identify consensus domains across slices missed by non-integration methods. For example, the laminar structure of layer 4 in two slices was missed by STAGATE and BayesSpace. In addition, we further tested the robustness of STAligner regarding read-depth variation by downsampling experiments on simulated DLPFC data, and found that STAligner showed robust clustering performance even when the gene expressions were downsampled at a very sparse level (downsampling rate < 0.5). We further compared the performance on a more challenging setting by integrating all 12 slices from the 3 samples. The Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) plot of unaligned embeddings produced by a tool developed for Single-Cell Analysis in Python (SCANPY) showed that the three samples exhibited substantial batch effects with common cortical layers from different samples separated. The embeddings of Harmony and SEDR were over-corrected with a disordered layer structure, whereas STAligner evenly mixed the spots from the shared layers across samples and made the layers organized as their spatial locations. This conclusion can be further confirmed by quantitative measure of batch-corrected embedding on both real and simulation datasets, where STAligner achieved a comparable divergence score but a much higher silhouette coefficient than competing methods. Moreover, the previously reported layer-marker genes, such as FABP7 (layer 1), HPCAL1 (layer 2), CARTPT (layer 3), PCP4 (layer 5), KRT17 (layer 6) and MBP (white matter), showed differential expressions among the identified layers. STAligner demonstrated more consistent spatial domains across samples and a higher median ARI score of 0.47, compared with Harmony (median ARI = 0.19) and SEDR (median ARI = 0.43). In addition, STAligner recognized the structure of layer 1 and layer 2 at the top right part of layer 3 in sample 3, which were missed when analyzing this sample alone and can be verified by the expression of marker gene VIM, demonstrating that STAligner can depict the shared tissue structures across different samples.

section: Results: Multi-Slice Stitching, Integration Across Technologies, Mouse Embryo Dynamics
text: Some ST technologies may be limited by the size of the captured area and cannot cover a whole organ of interest for some cases. As a solution, the tissue sample is dissected into multiple slices. We applied STAligner to a mouse brain dataset where the tissue was divided into the sagittal anterior and posterior slices. STAligner captured the known brain tissue structures of the olfactory bulb, cortex, striatum, thalamus, hypothalamus, hippocampus, cerebellum, midbrain, hindbrain and corpus callosum. In particular, STAligner accurately recognized three small structures in the hippocampus region, with one denoting the dentate gyrus structure, one denoting cornu ammonis 1 (CA1) and one denoting CA3. We also found that the tissue structures flanking the shared border of the anterior and posterior slice were accurately identified as the same domains, such as the structures CA1 and CA3, and the superficial and deep cortex layer, which were also characterized by some marker genes such as Fibcd1 in CA1, Cabp7 in CA3, Lamp5 in the superficial cortex layer and Fezf2 in the deep cortex layer. Thus, STAligner could stitch multiple tissue slices into a large one for deciphering its global structure. Improved integration on slices from different platforms: We applied STAligner to integrate two mouse olfactory bulb ST slices profiled by the Slide-seqV2 and Stereo-seq platforms with clear tissue structures but substantial batch effects. Harmony followed with Louvain clustering failed to recover the laminar organization and SEDR found some intermixed spatial clusters with unclear tissue structures. By contrast, STAligner can decipher the known tissue structures according to the annotated laminar structure in the DAPI-stained image and Allen Mouse Brain Atlas annotation from both slices, indicating these two platforms indeed capture most of the key tissue structures. The six shared spatial domains by the two slices were spatially ordered from the inner layers, middle layers to outer layers of the mouse olfactory bulb, that is, the granule cell layer (GCL), mitral cell layer (MCL), external plexiform layer (EPL), glomerular layer (GL) and olfactory nerve layer (ONL). Moreover, STAligner identified two slice-specific spatial structures, the accessory olfactory bulb (AOB) and the granular layer of the accessory olfactory bulb (AOBgr) in the Slide-seqV2 ST slice, which may be caused by different sampling locations. The UMAP visualization of STAligner embeddings showed that the slice-shared laminar structures were mixed and the slice-specific structures were separated. However, Harmony and SEDR cannot recover these two structures and reveal such differences between these two ST slices. Furthermore, these spatial domains identified by STAligner were characterized by known marker genes. For example, the expression of Fxyd6 showed a distinct spatial distribution on the recognized AOB domain, and the expression of the granular cell marker Atp2b4 showed a distinct spatial distribution on the recognized AOBgr domain. The narrow loop structure of the MCL was enriched in the spatial expression. These results demonstrate that STAligner can integrate slices with different tissue structure compositions and avoid overcorrection of the slice-specific spatial domains. Identifying developmental mouse embryo tissue structures: We applied STAligner to integrate four mouse embryo slices sampled at the time stages of E9.5, E10.5, E11.5 and E12.5 profiled by Stereo-seq to investigate the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of tissue structures during mouse organogenesis. Although the four slices have very different sizes and there are obvious batch effects, STAligner can effectively integrate the four slices in the embedding space and align the spatial domains, which showed very consistent localization spatially across the four embryonic stages. Each domain showed clear tissue structure and can be annotated based on the anatomic location using eHistology Kaufman annotations. To reconstruct the developmental trajectory of each tissue structure, we built connections between the same spatial domains in successive stages and generated an acyclic directed graph to reflect the ancestor–descendant relationships. According to the origination from the three germ layers during gastrulation, the annotated organs and tissues can be summarized into three parts, that is, ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm. Notably, the tissues originated from different germ layers also showed clear co-localization patterns based on the UMAP visualization. More importantly, the annotated tissues across the four stages were characterized with some known marker genes, such as Sox2 for the forebrain, Ina for the hindbrain, Tbr1 for the olfactory bulb, Six6 for the hypothalamus, Myl7 for the heart, Trf for the liver, Pax1 for the cartilage primordium and Myog for the muscle. All the major tissues and organs (for example, heart, liver and brain) were detected and consistent with the well-known anatomical structure of the organ. In particular, the heart was accurately identified with nearly unchanged proportions over the course of the developmental period, which confirmed the fact that the heart is the first organ formed and functioned in the early embryo. The liver occupied a small area at the earliest stage E9.5 and rapidly increased size in the following stages. Likewise, we observed prominent increase in the proportions of hindbrain and muscle, indicating the proliferation of these tissue structures during the four stages of organogenesis. In addition, STAligner can also delineate tissues with special shapes, such as the meninges with a continuous closed loop at late embryonic stages. STAligner can also detect small (proportion < 5%) structures, such as the hypothalamus and olfactory bulb, at later developmental stages (after E11.5), which were supported by known histological evidence. The hypothalamic neurogenesis starts at E11.5 and reaches the peak at E12.5, and the development of olfactory bulb initiated at E10.5 and cannot be detected macroscopically until E12.5. These results demonstrate that STAligner can integrate the ST slices of different developmental stages, facilitate the cross-stage comparison and deepen our understanding on the developmental dynamics of the anatomical structure changes during mouse organogenesis. With the accumulation of ST data in both human and mouse, it is interesting and useful to identify consensus regions between them, making translational studies easier. We compared the embryo slice in mouse (at E10.5) and human (at Carnegie stage (CS) 13) profiled by Stereo-seq and 10x Visium technology, respectively. We used a one-to-one homologous relationship and applied STAligner to these two slides directly. It is interesting to see that most tissue structures between them were conserved. We also found a species-specific region dermomyotome, which may be attributed to the difference in developmental speeds between mouse and human organogenesis.

section: Results: Disease Analysis, 3D Reconstruction, Performance, Methods Part 1
text: Deciphering AD-associated spatial domains: We applied STAligner to integrate two spatial transcriptomics datasets of normal and Alzheimer’s disease (AD) mouse hippocampus tissue profiled by Slide-seqV2, and identified 16 distinct spatial domains. Most spatial domains revealed clear spatial patterns and corresponded to anatomical structures of hippocampus in the Allen Mouse Brain Atlas. The UMAP visualization of STAligner embeddings showed that the common domains from the two slices were highly overlapping and slice-specific domains were separated. STAligner could capture shared tissue structures between these two conditions. For example, domains 4, 7 and 11 constructed a clear ‘cord-arrow-like’ structure in the hippocampal region in both the two slices. According to the Allen Mouse Brain Atlas, domain 4 (‘arrow-like’) corresponds to the dentate gyrus region, and domains 7 and 11 (‘cord-like’) correspond to the structures CA1 and CA3 of Ammon’s horn, respectively, which were supported by some known gene markers. For example, Lrrtm4 regulating excitatory synapse development and AMPA receptor trafficking in dentate gyrus granule cells was expressed in the dentate gyrus field. To find the differences between AD and normal tissues, we first computed the composition of each domain belonging to the AD or normal slice. We found that domains 0 and 14 were located at the central hippocampal region (except for the ‘cord-arrow-like’ structure), but showed clear different compositions between slices, where domain 14 was more enriched in the AD slice. From the UMAP visualization, we can observe that domain 14 showed a distinct position and most spots of it belonged to the AD slice, although it was close to domain 0. Importantly, the high Aβ plaque density in the vicinity of domain 14 indicated that it was highly colocalized with the amyloid deposition, which is one of the key hallmarks of AD. This evidence combined with the composition observations suggested that domain 14 was AD associated. Moreover, the highly expressed genes (significantly upregulated) in domain 14 compared with domain 0 included microglia marker genes and some of them were implicated with the AD risk. For instance, Csf1r is a key growth factor receptor for microglia and has a crucial role for its homeostasis and development; and Tyrobp was found to be markedly upregulated in Aβ-associated brain regions and could enhance phagocytic activity of microglia and clear Aβ deposits. An earlier study demonstrated that C1qa, C1qb and C1qc are strongly expressed for Aβ-associated microglia and could mediate synapse loss in AD. We also find that the spatial expression distribution of differentially expressed genes (DEGs; C1qc and Tyrobp) is localized near the position of Aβ accumulation, illustrating the potential effect of Aβ accumulation on gene expression. These DEGs were involved in many Gene Ontology (GO) terms such as gliogenesis, astrocyte development and microglial cell activation. These results demonstrate that STAligner could preserve true biological variations while removing batch effects, and untangle the dysregulated cellular network surrounding the Aβ deposition of AD. To demonstrate the performance of STAligner on heterogeneous tissues, we also applied it to three human breast cancer slices from different patients sequenced by the 10x Visium platform. We found that the identified domains were highly consistent with histological annotation regions of invasive carcinoma, carcinoma in situ and non-tumor. Moreover, domains 4, 10 and 11 were more slice specific, which may be due to the heterogeneity among patients with tumors. Further differential expression analysis found that domains 4 and 10 exhibited a strong spatial expression pattern of genes VTN1 and NRAS, respectively. VTN1 was recognized as immunosuppressive gene, indicating that domain 4 could be a region of immunosuppressive status and has high potential for cancer invasion. It has been reported that NRAS can mediate autocrine/paracrine signaling and promote cell invasiveness in breast cancer. We also found increased expression of the gene FCGR3B for domain 11. FCGR3B is a classical marker gene for natural killer cells or neutrophils, indicating that domain 11 could be a region of cancer growth limited by immune activities. These results shown that STAligner can delineate the difference in tumor substructures across tumor samples. Three-dimensional reconstruction of consecutive mouse hippocampus slices: For the same tissue, multiple consecutive adjacent tissue slices need to be manually registered by rigid rotation and translation for 3D tissue reconstruction. Here we demonstrated the capability of automatic registration of seven adjacent mouse hippocampus slices generated by Slide-seqV1+. It is challenging to integrate and register these slices due to the low transcript detection sensitivity of Slide-seqV1 and only partial overlap among these adjacent slices. STAligner could successfully integrate the seven mouse hippocampus slices, and recognize the shared structure of corpus callosum (domain 1), Ammon’s horn and the dentate gyrus (domain 3), and third ventricle (domain 4), which demonstrated high consistency with the hippocampus annotations in the Allen Mouse Brain Atlas. The UMAP visualization of the STAligner embeddings demonstrated that the spots across slices were mixed while the spatial domains were separated with a clear expression pattern. These tissue structures were further confirmed by the higher expressions of oligodendrocytes marker Plp1 (corpus callosum), Hpca (Ammon’s horn and the dentate gyrus) and choroid plexus marker Ttr (third ventricle). Due to the difference between sampling position in the experimental procedures, the tissues in some pucks were incomplete, resulting in the differences of the shape and distribution of the tissue structures across the seven tissue slices. However, embedding alignment does not mean geometrical registration. According to the orientation and position of domains shared across slices (domain 3), we can observe that the adjacent slices were misaligned. Intuitively, using a common domain with asymmetrical shape and enough overlapping areas could make the further registration process robust against noise. We used the domain 3 as a landmark domain, which has a clear ‘cord-arrow-like’ structure, has major role in memory and spatial navigation, and is a major site of adult neurogenesis. The MNNs in this domain identified by STAligner were selected as anchor pairs to guide the registration process using the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm. All adjacent slices were aligned sequentially with translation and rotation along the z axis. From the stacked 3D tissue with registered coordinates, we can observe that the orientation and position of the ‘cord-arrow-like’ structure were matched accordingly, resulting in a clear visual improvement of 3D spatial patterns. By contrast, PASTE, which was designed for 3D reconstruction of globally similar ST slices, cannot correctly align these slices with partial overlap and thus failed to reconstruct the 3D ‘cord-arrow-like’ structure. These results illustrate that the MNNs and spatial domains generated by STAligner could help to guide coordinate registration of adjacent slices and achieve promising 3D tissue reconstruction for diverse tissue slices. Evaluating the computational time and memory usage: The rapid development of ST technologies results in higher spatial resolution, larger field of view and larger number of spots. These large-scale datasets require the integration method to be memory and time efficient. We recorded the computational time and graphics processing unit (GPU) memory usage cost by STAligner on three datasets. When integrating the largest mouse hippocampus dataset with seven slices and more than 170,000 spots in total, STAligner is fast and takes only 8.5 min. We can also find that the GPU memory usage remains constant when increasing the number of slices, which makes STAligner suitable for analyzing large-scale ST datasets. Methods: Data preprocessing: STAligner takes gene expression and spatial coordinate information of multiple ST slices as input. For each slice, we normalize the raw gene expressions according to library size and log-transformed using the SCANPY package. Then, we select the top-5,000 highly variable genes. We take the intersection of the highly variable genes of all the input slices to ensure the spot features are shared across slices. Construction of the spatial neighbor graph: On the basis of the spatial coordinate information of all spots, we measure the similarities between spots by the Euclidean distance. Two spots are considered as neighbor if the distance between them is shorter than a pre-defined threshold r. Using the spatial neighbor relationship, we construct an undirected neighbor graph denoted as the adjacency matrix A, where $A_{i j}=1$ if and only if there is an edge between spot i and spot j. A is a symmetric matrix with self-loops added for each spot. We can adjust r so that each spot has 4–15 nearest neighbors on average for diverse ST scenarios and platforms. Spatially aware spot triplet learning: STAligner consists of two parts: the graph attention autoencoder for spatially aware embedding learning and the spot triplet learning for batch correction.

section: Methods Part 2: Model Details, Training, Comparisons, Data, and Code
text: Graph attention autoencoder: The graph attention autoencoder is an unsupervised representation learning framework using both graph structure information and node attributes. It employs the graph attention mechanism to adaptively determine the influence of spatial neighbor nodes on learning node representations and reduce the effect of the noise and missingness in the expression data. Specifically, the graph attention autoencoder includes an encoder and a decoder. Let L be the number of layers in the encoder and N be the number of spots in the slices. The output representation of the k th $(k\in\{1,2,\ldots, L-1\})$ encoder layer of spot $i\in\{1,2,\ldots, N\}$ is formulated as follows: $$ z_i^{(k)}=\sigma\left(\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}\alpha_{i j}^{(k)}\left(W^{(k)} z_j^{(k-1)}\right)\right),\qquad(1)$$ where $\mathcal{N}_{i}$ represents the neighbor set of spot i (including spot i itself), $\sigma$ is the nonlinear activation function, $W^{(k)}$ is the trainable weight matrix, $z_{i}^{(0)}$ is the initial gene expression $x_{i}$ of spot $i$. $\alpha_{i j}^{(k)}$ is set to make the relevance coefficients of spot i’s neighbors comparable: $$\alpha_{i j}^{(k)}=\frac{\exp\left(e_{i j}^{(k)}\right)}{\sum_{l\in N_{i}} \exp\left(e_{l l}^{(k)}\right)},$$ where $e_{i j}^{(k)}$ indicates the relevance of spot i to its neighboring spot j, and can be computed by: $$ e_{i j}^{(k)}=\operatorname{Sigmoid}\left(v^{s(k)^T}\left(W^{(k)} z_i^{(k-1)}\right)+v^{r(k)^T}\left(W^{(k)} z_j^{(k-1)}\right)\right),\qquad(3)$$ where $v^{s(k)}$ and $v^{r(k)}$ are the trainable weight parameters of the k th layer, and Sigmoid denotes the sigmoid activation function. The L th encoder layer does not use the attention mechanism and can be calculated by: $$ z_i^{(L)}=\sigma\left(W^{(L)} z_i^{(L-1)}\right),$$ which is considered as the final embedding of spot i. The number of layers of the decoder is the same as the encoder. To reconstruct expression features, the decoder reverses the process of learning latent embedding in the encoder. Using the output of the encoder as the input of the decoder, the $k$ th $(k\in\{2,\ldots, L-1, L\})$ decoder layer reconstructs the representation of spot i in layer $k-1$ as follows: $$\hat{z}_{i}^{(k-1)}=\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_{i}}\widehat{\alpha}_{i j}^{(k-1)}\sigma\left(\widehat{W}^{(k)}\hat{z}_{j}^{(k)}\right).\qquad(5)$$ Similar to the encoder, the attention mechanism is not used in the last layer: $$\hat{z}_{i}^{(0)}=\sigma\left(\widehat{W}^{(1)}\hat{z}_{i}^{(1)}\right).\qquad(6)$$ The output $\hat{z}_{i}^{(0)}$ is considered as the reconstructed expressions. To avoid overfitting, STAligner sets $\widehat{W}^{(k)}=W^{(k)^{T}}$ and $\hat{\alpha}_{i j}^{(k)}=\alpha_{i j}^{(k)}$ respectively. The objective function of the graph attention autoencoder is to maximize the similarity between input and reconstructed expressions, achieved by minimizing the mean square error loss: $$L_{rec}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left\|\hat{z}_{i}^{(0)}-x_{i}\right\|_{2}.\qquad(7)$$ Spot triplet learning: We aim to enforce the encoder to learn a batch-corrected embedding for multiple ST data via the triplet learning, which has shown superior performance in diverse fields including scRNA-seq dataset integration. We define the spot triplet consisting of an anchor, a positive spot and a negative spot. It should be pointed out that, to avoid potential batch effects and dropouts, the triplets are defined in the embedding space instead of original gene expression space. The formed anchor–positive pair is used to overcome the batch effect while the anchor–negative pair guides the alignment in a discriminative way. We use the mutual nearest neighbors (MNN) pairs to define the anchor and positive spot that have similar gene expression but are from two different slices. The negative spot is randomly sampled from the same slice as the anchor spot. As the total number of spots is usually much larger than the k-nearest neighbors of the anchor spot, the probability of the sampled negative spot locating in the k-nearest neighbors of the anchor is very low. This negative sampling strategy can accelerate the model training, especially in large-scale datasets. The triplet loss is used to minimize the distance between the anchor–positive pair and maximize the distance between the anchor–negative pair in the latent space z. It is formulated with a Euclidean distance function as follows: $$ L_{tri}=\frac{1}{N_{tri}}\sum_{(a, p, n)\in\mathcal{C}_{tri}}^{N_{tri}}\max\left(\left\|z_a^{(L)}-z_p^{(L)}\right\|_2-\left\|z_a^{(L)}-z_n^{(L)}\right\|_2+\tau, 0\right),\quad(8)$$ where a, p and n represent the indices of the anchor, positive and negative spots respectively, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{tri}}$ is the set of the identified spot triplets with size $N_{\text{tri}}$ and $\tau$ (default 1.0) is the margin used to enforce the distance between positive and negative pairs. Finally, the final objective is written as: $$ L=(1-\alpha) L_{rec}+\alpha L_{tri}.\qquad(9)$$ where $\alpha$ is a hyperparameter that control the contribution of $L_{\text{rec}}$ and $L_{\text{tri}}$ in the loss function, and is set by 0.5 by default. The effect of hyperparameter $\alpha$ on performance is shown in Supplementary Fig. 15b. We perform spot triplet detection and encoder training in an iterative manner to generate refined triplets and improved embeddings. We first detect spot triplets in the embedding space generated by the encoder from the last iteration, and then guide the embedding alignment process with the detected triplets. Here, the initial embeddings are generated without using the triplet loss. Landmark spatial domain guided consecutive slice registration and 3D tissue reconstruction: Registration of the spot coordinates of the adjacent slices is an important step for 3D tissue reconstruction. A typical registration process uses landmark points to guide the registration, including three steps: landmark point (key point) extraction, landmark point matching and the optimal transformation estimation. The Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm is a typical method to achieve rigid transformation (that is, translation and rotation) to register the coordinates from different coordinate systems to a common coordinate framework with given landmark point matching. However, it is challenging to directly apply the algorithm to adjacent slices due to partial overlapping and dropout noise. Instead of detecting the correspondences for each spot in the adjacent slices, we consider the MNN spots as landmark points that come from a selected landmark domain shared across slices. Here, the landmark domain should be asymmetrical and have enough overlapping areas to improve the robustness of the corresponding points. Using the coordinates of the MNN spots as the input of the ICP algorithm, we can obtain a transformation matrix that can be further used to map all the spots of the target to the reference slice. The registration process is performed in a sequential pairwise manner across all the adjacent ST slices. The overall architecture of STAligner: The encoder of STAligner consists of two layers with node dimensions set as 512 and 30, respectively, and the decoder is just the reverse of the encoder. The Adam optimizer is used to minimize the loss function with learning rate of 10^{-4}. We set the weight decay as 10^{-4} and the activation function as the exponential linear unit. We adopt the PyTorch Geometric library to implement the graph attention autoencoder. We train the model in two steps: the graph autoencoder model is first trained without the triplet loss (500 iterations by default) and generates the embeddings for triplet identification. Then, we alternate the model training and triplet refinement every 100 iterations until the fixed number of iterations reached (500 iterations by default). All hyperparameters were optimized based on the performance on the DLPFC data. Clustering: The spatial domains were identified by clustering algorithm with the embeddings from STAligner as input. For datasets with prior knowledge about the number of domain labels, the mclust clustering algorithm implemented by R package mclust (v5.4.9) was used. For datasets without prior knowledge, the Louvain algorithm implemented by SCANPY package (v1.9.1) was employed. The adjusted Rand index (ARI) is used to quantify the agreement between the clustering result and the original annotation. Comparison methods: We compared STAligner with the following integration methods: Harmony, SEDR and PASTE. We also used SCANPY to produce uncorrected embeddings of the combined raw input data, where principal component analysis (PCA) was used to perform dimensionality reduction and no integration method was used. All parameters were kept at default values. Data description: The 10x Visium human DLPFC dataset was collected from three independent adult samples. Each sample consists of four adjacent tissue slices. The manually annotated labels in the original study include white matter and six neocortical layers. We used these annotations as ground truth. Each slice contains ~4,000 spots, ranging from 3,498 to 4,789. The mouse brain dataset was profiled by 10x Visium platform and the tissue was divided into two slices in the sagittal direction. The anterior and posterior slice contains 2,695 and 3,355 spots, respectively. The adult mouse olfactory bulb dataset was profiled by Stereo-seq and Slide-seqV2, respectively. The Stereo-seq slice processed by an early study contains 19,109 spots. The Slide-seqV2 slice contains 20,139 spots. The early mouse embryo dataset consisting of four stages (E9.5, E10.5, E11.5 and E12.5) profiled by Stereo-seq, contains 5,913, 18,408, 30,124 and 51,365 spots, respectively. The ST data of normal and AD disease mouse hippocampus slices both profiled by Slide-seqV2 contain 19,285 and 15,092 spots, respectively. For the AD slice, an adjacent Aβ plaque staining image of the same brain tissue was also collected. The mouse hippocampus data consisting of seven adjacent slices profiled by Slide-seqV1 with the number of spots ranging from 14,860 to 32,895. Slide-seqV1 data are highly noisy and sparse with lower transcript detection sensitivity than Slide-seqV2. Thus, recovering the true biology patterns is more challenging. The three human breast cancer slices based on 10x Visium platform consist of 4,727, 3,798 and 4,992 spots. The human embryo slice was profiled by 10x Visium platform, containing 2,909 spots. A summary of these datasets can be found in Supplementary Table 1. Data and code availability: Source data for Figs. 2-6 is available with this paper. The datasets analyzed in this study are all from publicly available datasets. Specifically, the human DLPFC dataset can be accessed in the spatialLIBD package (http://spatial.libd.org/spatialLIBD). The mouse olfactory bulb tissue data generated by Stereo-seq and Slide-seqV2 platforms can be accessed from https://github.com/JinmiaoChenLab/SEDR_analyses and https://singlecell.broadinstitute.org/single_cell/study/SCP815, respectively. The mouse sagittal posterior and anterior brain data can be accessed at https://support.10xgenomics.com/spatial-gene-expression/datasets/1.0.0/V1_Mouse_Brain_Sagittal_Posterior and https://support.10xgenomics.com/spatial-gene-expression/datasets/1.0.0/V1_Mouse_Brain_Sagittal_Anterior, respectively. The mouse embryo data can be accessed at https://db.cngb.org/stomics/mosta/. The human embryo data can be accessed at https://heoa.shinyapps.io/code/. The human breast cancer data can be accessed at https://cf.10xgenomics.com/samples/spatial-exp/1.0.0/V1_Breast_Cancer_Block_A_Section_1/V1_Breast_Cancer_Block_A_Section_1_web_summary.html, https://cf.10xgenomics.com/samples/spatial-exp/1.2.0/V1_Human_Invasive_Ductal_Carcinoma/V1_Human_Invasive_Ductal_Carcinoma_web_summary.html and https://cf.10xgenomics.com/samples/spatial-exp/2.0.0/CytAssist_FFPE_Human_Breast_Cancer/CytAssist_FFPE_Human_Breast_Cancer_web_summary.html. The normal and Alzheimer’s disease mouse hippocampus data can be accessed at https://singlecell.broadinstitute.org/single_cell/study/SCP815 and https://singlecell.broadinstitute.org/single_cell/study/SCP1663, respectively. The Slide-seq 3D mouse hippocampus slice can be accessed at https://singlecell.broadinstitute.org/single_cell/study/SCP354/slide-seq-study. The annotation images from the Allen Mouse Brain Atlas can be accessed at https://mouse.brain-map.org/static/atlas. An open-source Python implementation of the STAligner package is available at GitHub: https://github.com/zhanglabtools/STAligner and Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.8315415.
